Foundations of Marine Engine Technology
Students will explore the different careers available in the field of marine engine technology.
Explore career opportunities in marine engine technology.
Describe the roles and functions of individuals engaged in marine engine technology.
Explore opportunities for employment in marine engine technology.
Explore opportunities for entrepreneurial endeavors.
Students will understand basic concepts and practices necessary for effective marine shop operation.
Demonstrate basic shop safety skills.
Identify and demonstrate US Coast Guard and US Boating safety rules and regulations.
Operate hand tools safely and properly.
Operate tools and precision instruments for observing, measuring, and manipulating equipment and materials.
Set up, use, and maintain power tools safely and properly.
Identify all applicable federal, state and local laws, OSHA and EPA regulations in the operation of a marine shop.
Students will learn about various fasteners and their applications.
Identify and use different grades and types of fasteners.
Measure the torque of various fasteners used in the marine engine industry.
Identify the properties and apply various sealants appropriately.
Students will describe shop management systems and procedures.
Describe inventory management procedures.
Operate personal computers and software to access inventory databases.
Demonstrate customer service skills.
Write customer service orders.
Students will use and interpret information from various industry literature sources.
Select and apply service manuals for all types of marine engines.
Interpret and use service bulletins.
Interpret and apply service bulletins for all brands and types of marine engines.
Select appropriate online parts information for model engine undergoing service.
Marine Electrical Systems (ACCT-MES)
Students will demonstrate knowledge of electromagnetic and electrical theory, circuit analysis, and electrical system diagnosis and repair.
Explain the basic principles of magnetism.
Explain basic electrical theory.
Define and create basic DC and AC circuits as well as describe their differences.
Locate and identify electrical circuit components.
Identify types of analog and digital meters used in marine electrical servicing.
Set up and use analog and digital voltmeters, ammeters, and ohmmeters.
Diagnose circuit problems using digital multi-meter to record voltage drop.
Measure and record circuit readings using a VOA meter.
Define Ohm’s Law and the relationship among voltage, amperage, and resistance.
Describe a series and a parallel circuit.
Analyze circuit problems using Ohm’s Law.
Identify stray voltage causes in salt and fresh water boating environments.
Students will safely demonstrate knowledge of battery concepts, diagnosis, maintenance, and repair.
Describe different types of batteries and their application.
Set up and use test equipment to troubleshoot batteries.
Safely perform battery state of charge test and determine necessary action.
Perform battery capacity test and confirm proper battery capacity for vessel application and then determine necessary action.
Perform battery charge.
Inspect, clean, fill and/or replace battery, battery cables, connectors, clamps, and hold downs.
Start a vessel using auxiliary power supply.
Identify electronic modules and other accessories that require re-initialization and code entry after battery disconnect.
Students will demonstrate knowledge of starting system concepts, diagnosis, maintenance, and repair.
Perform starter current draw tests and determine necessary action.
Perform starter circuit voltage drop tests and determine necessary action.
Inspect and test starter relays and solenoids and determine necessary action.
Remove and install starter in a vessel.
Inspect and test switches, connectors, and wires of starter control circuits and perform necessary action.
Differentiate between electrical and engine mechanical problems that cause a slow- crank or no-crank condition.
Demonstrate knowledge of charging system concepts, diagnosis, maintenance, and repair.
Diagnose charging system for the cause of undercharge, no-charge, and overcharge conditions.
Inspect, adjust, or replace generator (alternator) drive belts, pulleys, and tensioners and check pulley and belt alignment.
Perform charging circuit voltage drop tests and determine necessary action.
Perform charging system output test and determine necessary action.
Marine Engine Drive Systems (ACCT-MED)
Students will understand theory of operation as well as evaluate problems and repair a basic marine diesel or gas four stroke Internal combustion engine.
Explain the basic principles of operation for marine diesel and gas four-stroke internal combustion engines.
Identify types of four-stroke internal combustion diesel and gas engines.
Check engine oil level; check engine for oil, coolant, and fuel leaks (Engine Off).
Check engine starting/operation (including unusual noises, vibrations, exhaust smoke, etc.); record idle and governed rpm.
Check engine for oil, coolant, air, and fuel leaks (Engine Running).
Conduct test of four-stroke engine power head operation.
Diagnose, inspect, and service cylinder head and valve train problems.
Execute four cycle engine valve timing procedures.
Diagnose, inspect, and service camshafts, lifters, timing chain, sprockets and gears.
Explain block repair methods including honing, boring, and sleeving.
Communicate procedures for basic block salvaging.
Students will understand theory of operation as well as assess and repair a basic marine two stroke engine.
Explain the basic principles of operation for marine two-stroke internal combustion engines.
Analyze types of two-stroke internal combustion engines.
Perform and test two-stroke engine power head operation.
Diagnose and identify power head failure and their causes, including pre-ignition and detonation.
Diagnose, inspect, repair, and remove&replace cylinder and crank case assemblies, crank shafts, rod and piston assemblies, cylinder heads, and bearings and seals.
Examine two stroke engine power head operation.
Explain block repair methods including honing, boring, and sleeving.
Explain and apply basic powerhead salvaging procedures and repair damaged threads and install thread repair inserts.
Students will understand theory of operation of inboard and outboard drive systems, transom bracket servicing and repair of the upper and lower gear case.
Discuss the in-board drive system.
Describe power transmission for inboard diesel engines.
Discuss the basic principles of the transom assembly.
Diagnose, inspect, and repair the transom assembly.
Explain the upper gear case assembly, theory, and operation.
Disassemble, inspect, and reassemble upper gear and lower gear case assemblies.
Describe and perform shimming procedures and gear lash inspection.
Perform pressure and vacuum leak test.
Students will understand diesel and gas engine fuel systems including tank, line, filter, and pump and hose components, and explain their operation.
Demonstrate the operation of two and four cycle carburetors.
Describe diesel fuel injections systems.
Check fuel tanks, mountings, lines, caps, and vents.
Inspect throttle linkages and return springs.
Describe reed valve operation as used in two cycle engines.
Explain two and four cycle diesel and gas fuel pump operations.
Diagnose and inspect the different types of oil metering systems used in outboard motors.
Conduct a fuel systems pressure and vacuum test to diagnose fuel pump operation and to isolate boat fuel system problems.
Inspect boat fuel system components for leaks, wear, and damage.
Inspect water separator/fuel heater; replace fuel filter(s); prime and bleed fuel system.
Relate electronic fuel injection theory and operation.
Identify the major components of the diesel and gas fuel injection systems and explain their operation in two and four cycle fuel injection systems.
Calibrate components of a diesel and gas fuel injection system in accordance to manufacturer’s specifications.
Check air induction system: piping, charge air cooler, hoses, clamps, and mountings; check for air restrictions and leaks.
Service or replace air filter as needed; check and reset air filter restriction indicator.

Students will be able to explain the water cooling system from the intake ports through the water pump and through the cooling passages including the thermostats, manifolds, elbows, and other related cooling items for both two and four stroke diesel and gas engines.
Track water flow through cooling systems and utilize water flow diagrams.

Describe and diagnose overheating conditions on two and four cycle diesel and gas marine engines, disassemble, inspect, and reassemble inboard and outboard cooling system components, including thermostats, coolant hoses, clamps, bypass valves, water pump assembly and pressure relief components.
Inspect and service heat exchanger 6d. Inspect and service coolant filter/conditioner.
Disassemble, inspect, and reassemble stern drive cooling system components including thermostats, bypass valves, water pump assembly, and pressure relief components.
Check coolant for contamination, supplemental coolant additives (SCA) concentration, and protection level (freeze point).
Students will understand how to layout, measure, and install marine accessories.
Measure and install accessories utilizing holes aws, electrical connectors, and proper soldering procedures.
Apply Ohm’s Law in relation to measuring resistance in marine accessory systems.
Set up and program marine accessories to manufacturer’s specifications.

Logistics Operations
CFItems.notes:
**Course Description:** Logistics Operations is the second course in the Distribution and Logistics career pathway. Successful completion of this course along with Logistics Fundamentals will prepare students for the Certified Logistics Associate (CLA) exam. This course will introduce students to global supply chain logistics covering topics, such as the global logistics environment, the importance of planning and logistics strategies, customer service, material handling safety and operations, global supply chain operations, and quality control. Students will be instructed through the use of lecture, guided inquiry, project-based learning, and interviews with industry professionals, authentic learning experiences, teamwork, simulations, and problem solving. Students should
also participate in leadership development activities with the Career Technical Student Organizations (CTSOs). This course is a pre-requisite for the Materials Management course that is required in order to complete the Certified Logistics Technician (CLT) exam. The prerequisite for the course is Logistics Fundamentals.

**Document Date:** October 11, 2013

Basic Maintenance and Light Repair
CFItems.notes:
**Course Description:** This course is designed as the foundational course for the Automobile Maintenance and Light Repair pathway. Students in this course will learn the basic skills needed to gain employment as a maintenance and light repair technician. Students will be exposed to courses in automotive preventative maintenance and servicing and replacing brakes, and steering and suspension components. In addition, student will learn how to do general electrical system diagnosis, learn electrical theory, perform basic tests and determine necessary action. In addition, students will learn how to evacuate and recharge air-conditioning systems using the proper refrigerant. The hours completed in this course are aligned with ASE/NATEF standards and are a base for the entry-level technician. The pre-requisite for this course is advisor approval.
**Document Date:** January 25, 2013
